name: Kubernetes MCP Server
what_it_does: Kubernetes MCP server enabling AI agents to interact with Kubernetes clusters — managing pods, deployments, services, and namespaces; executing kubectl commands; querying cluster state; reading logs; and automating Kubernetes operations from agent-driven DevOps and SRE workflows.
use_cases: Querying pod status and cluster health from SRE monitoring agents
Executing rollouts and scaling deployments from deployment automation agents
Reading container logs for incident investigation from on-call agents
Managing Kubernetes resources (ConfigMaps, Secrets, Services) from platform agents
Running kubectl commands for cluster operations from DevOps agents
Validating deployment status and readiness from CI/CD agents
not_for: Teams using AWS ECS, Nomad, or other container orchestrators
Kubernetes cluster provisioning (use Terraform or Pulumi for infra-as-code)
Production cluster operations without proper RBAC — high blast radius
best_when: An agent needs to interact with a Kubernetes cluster for operations, monitoring, or deployment automation with kubectl-level access.
avoid_when: You don't use Kubernetes — or if you need cluster provisioning rather than cluster management.

auth.notes: Uses kubeconfig for authentication — inherits credentials from local kube context or mounted service account. RBAC controls what the agent can do in cluster.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Open source — free, runs against any Kubernetes cluster
pricing.paid_tiers: None (Kubernetes is open source; cloud Kubernetes costs depend on provider)
pricing.notes: Kubernetes itself is open source. MCP server is community open source. Infrastructure costs depend on your Kubernetes provider (EKS, GKE, AKS, self-hosted).

performance.rate_limits: Kubernetes API server rate limits apply (default: 400 req/s per client)

known_agent_gotchas: Inherits kubeconfig permissions — agent has same access as configured kube context
RBAC scoping critical — agents should use least-privilege service accounts
kubectl commands are destructive if misconfigured — agents need guardrails
Namespace-scoped vs cluster-scoped resources have different permission requirements
Log streaming requires long-running connections — timeout handling important
Multi-cluster support requires careful context switching — misconfiguration is risky
agent_readiness.security_notes: TLS enforced by Kubernetes API server. RBAC mandatory for agent service accounts. Community MCP server — use read-only service account in production. High blast radius — use restrictive RBAC.

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS via Kubernetes API server. RBAC for fine-grained access control. Community MCP server. Use least-privilege service account — k8s operations have high blast radius.
